text: Total mesocolic excision is a potential determinant of survival following oncological resection. The esplenic flexure of the colon extends from one-third of the left transverse colon to the first portion of the descending colon. The lymphatic drainage routes may be the left branch of the middle colic artery and left colic artery

The surgeon begins the dissection of the inferior mesenteric vein as part of the colonic splenic flexure mobilization. A peritoneal window is opened below the vein using blunt and sharp dissection techniques. The left colic artery and inferior mesenteric vein are dissected away from retroperitoneal structures and prepared for ligation. The left colic artery is centrally tied, and the inferior mesenteric vein is isolated and divided. The colon is reflected medially, and lateral attachments of the left colon are sectioned up to the splenic flexure. The omentum is taken en bloc with the colon, and the lesser sac is opened through the transgastrocolic ligament. The splenocolic and pancreatocolic ligaments are divided to complete the mobilization. The transverse colon is retracted to expose colopancreatic attachments, and the mesentery of the transverse colon is freed from medial to lateral. The left branch of the middle colic artery is identified and divided, completing the division of the mesocolon and the artery of Drummond.

The treatment of splenic flexure colon cancer is not standardized because the lymphatic drainage is variable. In this case, the central ligation of the major vessel feeding tumor and draining veins was performed based on a CT scan.

The patient is positioned in the modified lithotomy position on the operating table. The surgeon is positioned on the right side of the patient, while the cameraman is on the left side of the surgeon, and the first assistant is on the left side of the patient.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum under the inferior mesenteric vein and performs a blunt dissection to separate the mesocolon from the prurinal fat tissue. The lymph node dissection is carried out at the root of the inferior mesenteric vein. A blunt dissection beneath the inferior mesenteric artery exposes the origin of the left colic artery, where a central tie is applied.

The surgeon isolates and divides the inferior mesenteric vein below the inferior border of the pancreas. The lateral attachments of the colon are incised up to the splenic flexure, followed by the division of the frenocolic ligament.

The surgeon performs an oncologic dissection of the splenic flexure using an anterior approach. The greater omentum is divided close to the stomach, and the dissection continues leftward until the splenocolic ligament is divided. The patient is in the anti-Trendelenburg position, with the cameraman positioned between the patient's legs using a thirty-degree camera angle. Full mobilization of the splenic flexure is achieved without additional trocars.

The surgeon performs dissection of the left transverse mesocolon from the inferior border of the pancreas, dividing the pancreatic ligament. Using diathermy, the mesocolon is separated from the spleen and retroperitoneum. The middle colic vessels are identified, and the left branch is divided. A supraumbilical laparotomy incision is made through the umbilical port to remove the specimen. The marginal artery is divided in its transverse and left colonic portion. A GIA device is used to divide the large intestine, followed by a hands-on lateral-lateral colicolic anastomosis, completing the specimen removal.
Laparoscopic resection of colon cancer at splenic flexure is technically demanding, but once anatomical knowledge and technical tips are acquired, it could be feasible as minimally invasive surgery.
